summary: At school, I was quite strong in Sumo and was considered the third strongest in our school. I remember a young teacher named Mr. Kobayashi from Osaka who instructed me well in Sumo. It was a significant part of my youth and remains an old dream of mine. Sumo gave me strength and discipline during my early years in Japan.

In school, I learned about Kusunoki Masashige and was deeply impressed by his teaching to his son before the battle at Minatogawa. He said that a lion kicks its 3-day-old cub down a cliff to test its strength, and urged his son to rise up even in bad situations and defeat the enemy after his death. This lesson about courage and resilience stayed with me and supported my morale. It was one of the most memorable teachings I received during my education.

Minatogawa is the place where Kusunoki Masashige sent his son to battle, and it was there that he gave his famous instruction about strength and courage. I learned about this place in school as part of Japanese history. The story of Minatogawa and the battle connected deeply with me, symbolizing perseverance in difficult times. It represents a historical moment that taught me valuable life lessons.

My family practiced the Zen sect, and religion was an important part of our daily life. My grandparents were very religious and maintained traditions like offering cooked rice at the family altar before breakfast. They also forbade cooking meat in the house, showing their strict adherence to religious customs. I appreciate that kind of attitude and feel thankful for the values they instilled.

When I first came to America, our ship stopped at Vancouver, and I was impressed by the beautiful scenery. From the ship, I saw lawns along the beach and houses painted white and red, which looked like a picture to me. It was my first time seeing a foreign country, and Vancouver left a strong impression on me. It was a memorable part of my journey to the United States.

After Vancouver, we went to Seattle where I landed in the United States. I stayed at the Immigration Office there for two or three days before officially landing. The immigration process included an eye examination and a test where we had to read a piece of paper in Japanese. Seattle was my first landing point in America, and from there I took a ship to San Pedro.

When I arrived in San Pedro, Los Angeles, my father came to greet me. We stayed in Japantown there for one or two nights before I started working. I remember working hard clearing land using horses and digging out rocks. San Pedro was where I began my life in America, and it was a tough but better time compared to later days.

Japantown in Los Angeles was where I stayed briefly after arriving in San Pedro. It was a place where many Japanese gathered, but I felt the reputation of the community was not very good because of the concentration of people. Despite that, it was a center for Japanese life and culture, and I remember the Tokyo Club gambling house there. Japantown was an important part of my early experience in America.

I worked at orange ranches in Riverside, California, where Japanese bosses gathered 20 to 30 farm laborers. These camps were common in those days but no longer exist now. I earned wages picking oranges and lived a routine life of early mornings and hard work. Riverside was a significant place for me during my early years in America as a farm laborer.

The Tokyo Club was a gambling house located in Japantown, San Pedro, where many single Japanese men spent time. Gambling was common among single men then, and some led fast lives because of it. I remember the Tokyo Club as part of the social scene during those days, though I personally focused more on work. It was a notable place in the Japanese community at the time.

The Immigration Act of 1924 had a big impact on the Japanese community here. Before 1924, many Japanese women came to America, but after the law took effect, immigration was restricted. I started going to church around 1924, and the Japanese community was still small then. The act shaped the demographic and social life of Japanese immigrants during that time.

Osaka is where Mr. Kobayashi, a young teacher who instructed me well in Sumo, came from. His teaching left a lasting impression on me during my school days in Japan. Though I lived in a quiet village, the connection to Osaka through my teacher was significant in my early education. Osaka represents a link to my learning and growth in sports and discipline.
